title: Raised gesso for gold-leaf illumination
subtitle: Making and applying the chalk ground that raises gold proud of the page
excerpt: In illuminated manuscripts, gold leaf sits not flat on the page but raised on a chalk-and-sugar gesso ground that gives the gold its characteristic three-dimensional glow. The gesso is mixed from dry pigment and organic binders, applied in layers, allowed to dry, and then lightly dampened to receive the leaf. A demanding technique, but the only one that produces the genuine medieval look.
sourceNotes: Edward Johnston, Writing and Illuminating and Lettering (1906), chapter on gilding, Johnston gives a detailed gesso recipe and application procedure that remains the standard reference. Cennino Cennini, Il Libro dell'Arte (c. 1400, first printed 1821), the original medieval source for gesso recipes; PD in translation. Internet Archive editions of both available.

glossaryTerms.definition: A chalk-and-lead-white ground mixed with sugar and organic binder, applied to vellum or paper in layers to create a raised cushion that supports gold leaf and gives it a curved reflective surface.
Clarified egg white used as a binder in manuscript illumination. Made by beating egg white to a stiff meringue, leaving it overnight, then straining the clear liquid.
Polishing laid gold leaf with an agate burnisher to compress the gold and create a mirror-bright reflective surface.

content.text: Raised gesso

is the most technically demanding element of the manuscript illumination tradition. The recipe is straightforward; the practice takes weeks to become reliable. Johnston spent months refining his gesso before he was happy with the results. Expect the first several attempts to be uneven, and know that even experienced illuminators sometimes lose a gold-laid panel to a bubble or a collapse.
What you need

items.name: Manuscript raised gesso (dry mix)
items.qty: one small pot, used in small batches

items.name: Glair (clarified egg white) or PVA solution (1:20 with distilled water) as binder
items.substitutions: For practice work, diluted PVA works; for period-accurate illumination, glair is correct

items.name: Calfskin vellum
items.qty: small practice piece, at least A5
items.substitutions: Smooth hot-press watercolour paper at 300 gsm for practice; vellum for finished work

items.name: Soft brush, size 0 or 1 (sable if possible)

content.text: Mixing the gesso
Place a small amount of dry gesso mix in a ceramic saucer or palette. Add
glair
(or dilute PVA solution) drop by drop, mixing with a brush until the consistency is like double cream, flowing slowly when the brush is lifted, not dripping.
The commercial gesso mix already has the right amount of sugar. Do not add extra; too much sugar stops the surface from taking the gold cleanly.
Applying the gesso layers
Transfer the mixed gesso to the design area with a fine brush. Fill the shape, working from the edges inward to avoid flooding the outline.
Allow the first layer to dry completely, 30 minutes in a dry room. Apply a second layer, slightly narrower than the first, to build height in the centre. The gesso should dome slightly, not be flat.
Apply a third layer if more height is needed. Typical finished height is 0.3 to 0.5 mm proud of the vellum surface.
Leave to dry for at least 2 hours, ideally overnight.
Laying the gold

Cut a piece of loose gold leaf slightly larger than the gesso shape. Work in still air, gold leaf lifts on a breath. Breathe across the gesso from a distance of 5 cm for 2 to 3 seconds; the moisture in your breath re-activates the sugar and makes the surface tacky.
Immediately lower the gold leaf onto the gesso, tissue backing uppermost if using transfer leaf. Press gently with a piece of glassine paper over the top.
Apply a second piece of leaf to cover any gaps, gaps appear as dark patches. Multiple layers are not a problem; they can all be burnished together.
Burnishing

Wait 30 minutes after laying the gold before burnishing. The gesso must be fully firm. Work the agate burnisher in small circles over the gold, starting at the edges and working inward, increasing pressure gradually. The gold will transform from a matte yellow to a mirror-bright reflective surface. Stop when the gold no longer moves under the burnisher.
Common mistakes

items.symptom: Gold does not stick to the gesso
items.cause: The gesso is too dry, or the breath moisture was insufficient.
items.fix: Breathe directly onto the gesso from closer, 2 to 3 cm, for slightly longer, or hold a small piece of wet cloth near but not touching the gesso for 10 seconds before laying.

items.cause: The burnisher pressure is too high too soon, or the gesso has not fully hardened.
items.fix: Wait longer before burnishing. Begin with the lightest possible pressure and increase very gradually.

items.symptom: Gesso collapses or develops cracks while drying
items.cause: Too thick a layer applied in one pass, or too much binder in the mix.
items.fix: Build in thinner layers. If large cracks appear, scrape back and start again with a thinner mix.
